channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Two Critically Injured After Gunman Fires Into Traffic in Cambridge
item.description: As people fled their cars along the busy riverfront road, a state trooper and an armed civilian ran toward the gunman and exchanged fire with him.
item.dc:creator: Jin Yu Young
item.media:description: Police officers respond to a man who had been seen shooting a weapon along Memorial Drive in Cambridge, Massachusetts, on Monday.

item.title: Instructure Strikes Deal for Hackers for Return of Canvas Data
item.description: Instructure, which provides Canvas software to thousands of schools and universities around the world, did not say what it had given the hackers in exchange for the stolen data.
item.dc:creator: Qasim Nauman
item.media:credit: Alessandro Penso for The New York Times
item.media:description: Hackers stole data from Canvas, an education platform used by thousands of schools and universities around the world.

item.title: Judge Again Delays Guantánamo’s First Death-Penalty Terror Trial
item.description: The 2000 terrorism case has been going on for so long that the parents of fallen sailors and shipmates who survived the attack have died.
item.dc:creator: Carol Rosenberg
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: The U.S.S. Cole bombing case is on track to become the first capital trial at Guantánamo Bay. Jury selection was again delayed.

item.title: Justice Dept. Subpoenas Wall Street Journal in Leak Investigation
item.description: Critics raised concerns over press freedom after The Journal disclosed receiving subpoenas related to an article on deliberations on the risks of military action in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:description: The Wall Street Journal headquarters in New York. The inquiry into the origins of The Journal’s reporting is one of multiple leak investigations being conducted by the U.S. attorney’s office in the Eastern District of Virginia, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: Kari Lake, Who Led Effort to Shutter Voice of America, Is Named Ambassador to Jamaica
item.description: Ms. Lake tried and failed to fire hundreds of Voice of America journalists and to cut funding for other federally funded news groups like Radio Free Europe/Radio Liberty.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.category: United States Agency for Global Media
item.media:credit: Maansi Srivastava for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kari Lake speaking at the annual CPAC event in Oxon Hill, Md., last year.

item.title: Arcadia Mayor Eileen Wang Will Plead Guilty to Working as Chinese Agent
item.description: Eileen Wang, who resigned Monday as the mayor of Arcadia, Calif., published propaganda on a purported news site under direction from Chinese officials, prosecutors said.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:description: Eileen Wang at the Asian Hall of Fame induction ceremony in Los Angeles in 2023.

item.title: Inside the Hantavirus Quarantine Unit in Nebraska Where 15 Americans Wait
item.description: The National Quarantine Unit in Nebraska is now the temporary home for many of the Americans who had been passengers on the MV Hondius.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao
item.media:description: The Davis Global Center, at the University of Nebraska Medical Center campus in Omaha, houses the National Quarantine Unit.

item.title: Supreme Court Clears Path for Alabama to Use New Voting Map
item.description: A majority of the justices sided with Alabama in a move that could speed up efforts to put in place a congressional district map that would eliminate a majority-Black district.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle and Emily Cochrane

item.title: Senate G.O.P. Stands by Security Money for Trump’s Ballroom
item.description: Democrats are trying to stoke public opposition to the project as they take aim at legislation to provide nearly $72 billion for the administration’s immigration crackdown.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:description: Construction continued on the East Wing last month.

item.title: How Democratic Divisions Are Playing Out in NYC’s Primary Races
item.description: Establishment and left-wing leaders are clashing over House seats scattered across the city.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Fandos
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Democrats are clashing over a number of House seats across New York City.

item.title: Why Republicans Are Still Drawing House Maps, While Democrats Are Stuck
item.description: The G.O.P. has seized an edge on redistricting thanks to two court rulings, and it has more room to maneuver for extra seats before the midterms.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Brad J. Vest for The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Steve Cohen of Tennessee and State Senator Raumesh Akbari, both Democrats, discussing their state’s new House map, which is meant to unseat Mr. Cohen.

item.title: Supreme Court Continues Access to Abortion Pill by Mail, for a Few Days
item.description: Justice Samuel A. Alito Jr. has paused until at least Thursday a federal appeals court ruling against the Food and Drug Administration that would restrict access by mail to mifepristone.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The justices acted in response to a request from two mifepristone manufacturers that asked the Supreme Court to intervene this month after the conservative U.S. Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit restricted access to the pills.

item.title: Virginia Officials Ask Supreme Court to Restore Voting Map Drawn by Democrats
item.description: State officials asked the justices to overturn a Virginia Supreme Court decision that struck down a congressional map, a major defeat for Democrats.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle and Campbell Robertson
item.media:description: The Virginia Supreme Court ruled last week that Democratic state lawmakers violated Virginia’s Constitution in enacting a voting map aimed at increasing the number of Democrats in U.S. House seats.

item.title: Alabama Opens Its Own Investigation Into the Southern Poverty Law Center
item.description: The law center, based in Montgomery, has drawn the ire of conservatives in recent years. Last month, the Justice Department charged the group with financial crimes.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:description: The Southern Poverty Law Center was founded in 1971, eventually earning its reputation as a civil rights organization when it battled the Ku Klux Klan in court.

item.title: The Voting Rights Decision Might Silence Black People in Fayette County, Tennessee
item.description: Black residents, who make up 25 percent of Fayette County, Tenn., won a new electoral map last year to change an all-white board of commissioners. They worry the Supreme Court’s ruling could threaten that.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:credit: Rory Doyle for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I’m just wondering, how long is it going to take Fayette County to pounce on the ruling?” said Wendell Wainwright, 75, who has lived there all his life.

item.title: Trump Proposes Suspending Federal Gas Tax Until Prices Fall
item.description: Congress would have to agree to any suspension, and dropping federal taxes would not make up for the steep increase in prices since the war began.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Gas prices have risen about 50 percent since the United States and Israel began the war with Iran on Feb. 28.

item.title: Laredo Officials Investigating Deaths of 6 People Found in Train Boxcar
item.description: Officials in Laredo, Texas, said at least one of the six people found inside the Union Pacific Railroad boxcar was from Mexico as investigators worked to identify the others.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval
item.media:description: A cargo train coming from Mexico crosses the border into the U.S., as seen from Laredo, Texas, in 2019. Investigators with the Mexican Examiner’s office in the border city of Laredo said on Monday that at least one of the six people found dead inside a train boxcar was from Mexico.

item.title: Trump to Be Joined by Elon Musk and Other CEOs at Xi Summit in China
item.description: The delegation includes business leaders across a wide range of industries, including Tim Cook of Apple and Elon Musk of Tesla.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager

item.title: Body of U.S. Soldier Is Found Off the Coast of Morocco
item.description: First Lt. Kendrick Lamont Key Jr., 27, of Richmond, Va., was said to be on a hike at a training range when he disappeared. A second service member is still missing.
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: First Lt. Kendrick Lamont Key, Jr. was in Morocco for joint training exercises when he disappeared.

item.title: Man Charged With Assassination Attempt at Correspondents’ Dinner Pleads Not Guilty
item.description: Cole Tomas Allen faces four counts in what prosecutors say was a plot to kill President Trump and other top officials at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, and Jeanine Pirro, the U.S. attorney for the District of Columbia, at a news conference last month about the shooting. The defense lawyer asked that they be recused from the case.

item.title: Reflecting Pool Repairs to Cost $13.1 Million. Trump Had Promised $1.8 Million.
item.description: The Interior Department added $6.2 million to the no-bid contract for repairs to the Lincoln Memorial pool late last week.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold and Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Atlantic Industrial Coatings was hired to repair leaking joints between the pool’s concrete slabs, waterproof the pool’s bottom and paint it a shade called “American flag blue.”

item.title: Google Says Criminal Hackers Used A.I. to Find a Major Software Flaw
item.description: The company said that it had identified, for the first time, hackers using artificial intelligence to discover an unknown bug. The attempted attack represents “a taste of what’s to come,” one expert said.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz
item.media:description: “We have high confidence that the actor likely leveraged an A.I. model to support the discovery and weaponization of this vulnerability,” Google said in its report, published Monday.

item.title: Dua Lipa Sues Samsung Over Use of Her Image on TV Packaging
item.description: The pop star is suing the electronics giant for more than $15 million, accusing it of using her image without authorization.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.media:credit: Jean Baptiste Lacroix/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Dua Lipa at an event in Los Angeles in March.

item.title: Lawmakers Urge Trump to Move Ahead on Delayed Arms Sale to Taiwan
item.description: The Trump administration has held up the sale for months ahead of President Trump’s meeting this week with President Xi Jinping of China.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.media:description: From left, Senators Jeanne Shaheen, John Curtis, Thom Tillis and Jacky Rosen in Taiwan in March. All four signed a letter to President Trump urging him to move forward with the Taiwan arms sale.

item.title: Scientists Press Congress on Dismissal of National Science Foundation Board and Research Funding
item.description: The slowed pace of grants by the National Science Foundation, under attack from the White House, could put the United States at a disadvantage with China, the scientists warned.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella
item.media:description: The headquarters of the National Science Foundation in May last year, before the Trump administration announced it was moving the agency out of the building.

item.title: ‘It’s Getting Unfriendlier’: International Students Race to Find Jobs
item.description: Students say they have been passed over for jobs and interviews because of visa restrictions. Some have a Plan B: leaving the United States.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin and Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: Tony Luong for The New York Times
item.media:description: “It’s just getting unfriendlier and unfriendlier,” said Caroline Liu, a Chinese citizen and student at M.I.T.

item.title: Accusations of Shenanigans Fly in Nebraska’s Senate Race
item.description: One Democrat is accused of being a Republican in disguise. Another plans to drop out to boost an independent. It’s primary eve in one of the country’s most unusual midterm contests.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning
item.media:credit: Walker Pickering for The New York Times
item.media:description: The erosion of the Democratic Party brand in the Midwest and Great Plains has led to independent candidates’ sometimes mounting more credible campaigns in rural states like Nebraska.

item.title: Kansas City, a Car Town, Spends Millions on World Cup Transit
item.description: Security and other expenses are piling up for host cities. In the smallest metro area staging games, the bill includes temporary bus systems in two states.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: David Robert Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Officials in Kansas City are working to ensure that a long-planned extension of the city’s streetcar system is completed ahead of the World Cup.

item.title: They Were Promised New Septic Tanks. Trump Called It ‘Illegal DEI.’
item.description: The Justice Department ended a deal that had helped fund a solution to the sewage crisis in rural Alabama. “Almost like we are starting all over again,” one activist said.
item.dc:creator: Bernard Mokam
item.media:credit: Nicole Craine for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pipe carrying sewage away from a home in Hayneville, Ala.

item.title: Cursive Club, Where Students Learn With a Flourish
item.description: Years after it was omitted from the Common Core standards, some students are practicing cursive in clubs after school and in libraries. Some states are bringing it back to classrooms.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk
item.media:credit: Emon Hassan for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chris Kobara practices writing his name in a cursive writing club at the Urban Assembly Early College High School of Emergency Medicine in Manhattan.
